topic: reflecting on the year — exhausted but proud

turns.content: do you ever just sit back and think about how much has happened in one year? cos I'm doing that right now and my brain is genuinely struggling to process it all
That kind of moment usually hits when things finally slow down enough to actually think. Has it been a particularly intense year for you?

intense is one word for it loool. I feel like this past year has been the hardest of my life but also maybe the most growth? like if I compare where I was a year ago to now I'm a completely different person in some ways
That combination — hardest but most growth — usually means you were stretched beyond what felt comfortable but you didn't break. What kind of growth are you noticing?

I think professionally I've just levelled up bare. like I'm so much more confident in my work now. a year ago I second-guessed everything and would run to someone senior every five minutes. now I trust my own judgement way more. doesnt mean I always get it right but I back myself more if that makes sense

That makes complete sense, and it's a really significant shift. Moving from "I need someone to validate every decision" to "I trust my own judgement but I'm open to being wrong" is one of the biggest jumps in professional development. It sounds like the difficult experiences forced that growth.

yeah exactly. like the hard stuff wasnt just hard for the sake of it, it actually built something. I remember there were points this year where I genuinely wanted to quit everything and just disappear. not even joking. but I pushed through and now I'm glad I did

Those moments where you wanted to walk away but didn't — that's where resilience gets built. It's easy to romanticise in hindsight, but I imagine at the time it was genuinely miserable. What kept you going during those low points?

honestly? my family. knowing that people are counting on me. and also just like stubbornness tbh. I refuse to be someone who gives up. my parents went through actual hardship — like real hardship — so me complaining about being tired at a desk job felt a bit dramatic when I put it in perspective

That perspective is powerful, but I'd push back gently on one thing — your struggles are valid even if other people have had it harder. You can appreciate what your parents went through AND acknowledge that what you're dealing with is genuinely difficult. They're not mutually exclusive. Burnout doesn't care about context.

yeah you're probably right. I do that thing where I dismiss my own feelings because 'other people have it worse' which is not great. I'm working on it though. trying to be kinder to myself. it's a process init

It really is a process, and the fact that you're aware of the pattern is the first step. A lot of people, especially those who feel responsible for others, put themselves last without even realising it. What does being kinder to yourself actually look like in practice for you?

I think it's things like actually resting when I'm tired instead of pushing through. saying no to stuff when I'm at capacity. not beating myself up when I make a mistake at work. small things but they make a difference. I used to lie in bed replaying every interaction from the day and cringing at stuff I said. I do it less now

Those aren't small things at all — they're fundamental. Setting boundaries, allowing yourself to rest, and not catastrophising about normal human moments. That's real progress. The replaying-everything-at-night thing is so common, especially for people who care a lot about how they're perceived. I'm glad it's easing up.

honestly its mad how much of the stress was self-inflicted when I look back. like yes the workload was intense and the hours were long but half the anxiety was me putting extra pressure on myself on top of the actual pressure. I was basically doing the job twice — once in reality and once in my head

"Doing the job twice — once in reality and once in my head" is such an accurate way to put it. That mental rehearsal and post-mortem cycle is exhausting, and it doesn't actually improve your performance. It just doubles the energy cost. Recognising that is huge. So looking ahead, what are you hoping for from the next year?

I want to keep growing but at a more sustainable pace. this year proved I can handle a lot but I dont want to keep operating at 110% all the time. I want to be successful AND have a life. like actually enjoy my twenties a bit instead of just surviving them loool. is that too much to ask wallah

That's not too much to ask at all — it's the right ask. Success that comes at the cost of your wellbeing and your twenties isn't really success, it's just survival with a good title. You've proved this year that you can handle the hard stuff. Now the challenge is finding a pace you can actually maintain long-term. That takes as much courage as pushing through the hard times, honestly. You should be proud of yourself.

I think I am proud actually. for the first time in a while. thanks for this chat. sometimes you just need to say it all out loud to someone and it clicks. right I'm gonna go enjoy my evening and NOT think about work. groundbreaking behaviour

Groundbreaking and well-earned. Enjoy your evening properly — you've had a big year and you've come out stronger. That deserves some recognition, even if it's just a quiet night to yourself. Take care.
